Say Hello to the 100 Trillion Bacteria That Make Up Your Microbiome - NYTimes.com

Say Hello to the 100 Trillion Bacteria That Make Up Your Microbiome - NYTimes.com

NY Times · by Michael Pollan · May 15, 2013

I can tell you the exact date that I began to think of myself in the first-person plural \xe2\x80\x94 as a superorganism, that is, rather than a plain old individual human being. It happened on March 7. That\xe2\x80\x99s when I opened my e-mail to find a huge, processor-choking file of charts and raw data from a laboratory located at the BioFrontiers Institute at the University of Colorado, Boulder. As part of a new citizen-science initiative called the American Gut project, the lab sequenced my microbiome \xe2\x80\x94 that is, the genes not of \xe2\x80\x9cme,\xe2\x80\x9d exactly, but of the several hundred microbial species with whom I share this body. These bacteria, which number around 100 trillion, are living (and dying) right now on the surface of my skin, on my tongue and deep in the coils of my intestines, where the largest contingent of them will be found, a pound or two of microbes together forming a vast, largely uncharted interior wilderness that scientists are just beginning to map.

I clicked open a file called Taxa Tables, and a colorful bar chart popped up on my screen. Each bar represented a sample taken (with a swab) from my skin, mouth and feces. For purposes of comparison, these were juxtaposed with bars representing the microbiomes of about 100 \xe2\x80\x9caverage\xe2\x80\x9d Americans previously sequenced.

Here were the names of the hundreds of bacterial species that call me home. In sheer numbers, these microbes and their genes dwarf us. It turns out that we are only 10 percent human: for every human cell that is intrinsic to our body, there are about 10 resident microbes \xe2\x80\x94 including commensals (generally harmless freeloaders) and mutualists (favor traders) and, in only a tiny number of cases, pathogens. To the extent that we are bearers of genetic information, more than 99 percent of it is microbial. And it appears increasingly likely that this \xe2\x80\x9csecond genome,\xe2\x80\x9d as it is sometimes called, exerts an influence on our health as great and possibly even greater than the genes we inherit from our parents. But while your inherited genes are more or less fixed, it may be possible to reshape, even cultivate, your second genome.

Justin Sonnenburg, a microbiologist at Stanford, suggests that we would do well to begin regarding the human body as \xe2\x80\x9can elaborate vessel optimized for the growth and spread of our microbial inhabitants.\xe2\x80\x9d This humbling new way of thinking about the self has large implications for human and microbial health, which turn out to be inextricably linked. Disorders in our internal ecosystem \xe2\x80\x94 a loss of diversity, say, or a proliferation of the \xe2\x80\x9cwrong\xe2\x80\x9d kind of microbes \xe2\x80\x94 may predispose us to obesity and a whole range of chronic diseases, as well as some infections. \xe2\x80\x9cFecal transplants,\xe2\x80\x9d which involve installing a healthy person\xe2\x80\x99s microbiota into a sick person\xe2\x80\x99s gut, have been shown to effectively treat an antibiotic-resistant intestinal pathogen named C. difficile, which kills 14,000 Americans each year. (Researchers use the word \xe2\x80\x9cmicrobiota\xe2\x80\x9d to refer to all the microbes in a community and \xe2\x80\x9cmicrobiome\xe2\x80\x9d to refer to their collective genes.) We\xe2\x80\x99ve known for a few years that obese mice transplanted with the intestinal community of lean mice lose weight and vice versa. (We don\xe2\x80\x99t know why.) A similar experiment was performed recently on humans by researchers in the Netherlands: when the contents of a lean donor\xe2\x80\x99s microbiota were transferred to the guts of male patients with metabolic syndrome, the researchers found striking improvements in the recipients\xe2\x80\x99 sensitivity to insulin, an important marker for metabolic health. Somehow, the gut microbes were influencing the patients\xe2\x80\x99 metabolisms.

Our resident microbes also appear to play a critical role in training and modulating our immune system, helping it to accurately distinguish between friend and foe and not go nuts on, well, nuts and all sorts of other potential allergens. Some researchers believe that the alarming increase in autoimmune diseases in the West may owe to a disruption in the ancient relationship between our bodies and their \xe2\x80\x9cold friends\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x94 the microbial symbionts with whom we coevolved.

These claims sound extravagant, and in fact many microbiome researchers are careful not to make the mistake that scientists working on the human genome did a decade or so ago, when they promised they were on the trail of cures to many diseases. We\xe2\x80\x99re still waiting. Yet whether any cures emerge from the exploration of the second genome, the implications of what has already been learned \xe2\x80\x94 for our sense of self, for our definition of health and for our attitude toward bacteria in general \xe2\x80\x94 are difficult to overstate. Human health should now \xe2\x80\x9cbe thought of as a collective property of the human-associated microbiota,\xe2\x80\x9d as one group of researchers recently concluded in a landmark review article on microbial ecology \xe2\x80\x94 that is, as a function of the community, not the individual.

s sometimes called, exerts an influence on our health as great and possibly even greater than the genes we inherit from our parents. But while your inherited genes are more or less fixed, it may be possible to reshape, even cultivate, your second genome. Justin Sonnenburg , a microbiologist at Stanford, suggests that we would do well to begin regarding the human body as \xe2\x80\x9can elaborate vessel optimized for the growth and spread of our microbial inhabitants.\xe2\x80\x9d This humbling new way of thinking about the self has large implications for human and microbial health, which turn out to be inextricably linked. Disorders in our internal ecosystem a loss of diversity, say, or a proliferation of the \xe2\x80\x9cwrong\xe2\x80\x9d kind of microbes may predispose us to obesity and a whole range of chronic diseases, as well as some infections. \xe2\x80\x9c Fecal transplants ,\xe2\x80\x9d which involve installing a healthy person\xe2\x80\x99s microbiota into a sick person\xe2\x80\x99s gut, have been shown to effectively treat an antibiotic- resistant intestinal pathogen named C. difficile, which kills 14,000 Americans each year. (Researchers use the word \xe2\x80\x9cmicrobiota\xe2\x80\x9d to refer to all the microbes in a community and \xe2\x80\x9cmicrobiome\xe2\x80\x9d to refer to their collective genes.) We\xe2\x80\x99ve known for a few years that obese mice transplanted with the intestinal community of lean mice lose weight and vice versa. (We don\xe2\x80\x99t know why.) A similar experiment was performed recently on humans by researchers in the Netherlands: when the contents of a lean donor\xe2\x80\x99s microbiota were transferred to the guts of male patients with metabolic syndrome, the researchers found striking improvements in the recipients\xe2\x80\x99 sensitivity to insulin, an important marker for metabolic health. Somehow, the gut microbes were influencing the patients\xe2\x80\x99 metabolisms. Our resident microbes also appear to play a critical role in training and modulating our immune system, helping it to accurately distinguish between friend and foe and not go nuts on, well, nuts and all sorts of other potential allergens. Some researchers believe that the alarming increase in autoimmune diseases in the West may owe to a disruption in the ancient relationship between our bodies and their \xe2\x80\x9cold friends\xe2\x80\x9d the microbial symbionts with whom we coevolved. These claims sound extravagant, and in fact many microbiome researchers are careful not to make the mistake that scientists working on the human genome did a decade or so ago, when they promised they were on the trail of cures to many diseases. We\xe2\x80\x99re still waiting. Yet whether any cures emerge from the exploration of the second genome, the implications of what has already been learned for our sense of self, for our definition of health and for our attitude toward bacteria in general are difficult to overstate. Human health should now \xe2\x80\x9cbe thought of as a collective property of the human- associated microbiota,\xe2\x80\x9d as one group of researchers recently concluded in a landmark review article on microbial ecology that is, as a function of the community, not the individual. Thank you for your patience while we verify access. Already a subscriber? Log in . Want all of The Times? Subscribe .
